###  

1. # **Introduction** {#introduction}

As a hobby, many residents like to look after small plants in their home, whether it be to maintain a degree of sustainability in their kitchen or just wanting to take care of something that brings their home warmth and comfort. However, for the average user, it may be a little hard to maintain these plants if they are not all too familiarized with their plant’s distinct needs. They could over-hydrate the plan, leave it in the sun for too long, or simply forget to water it. That's where LeafLink comes in, a project designed for the course of Embedded Systems that aims to facilitate the user’s personal plant experience by offering a better monitoring system for their plant. This will be done by first and foremost facilitating the most important information to the user, like the soil’s humidity level, and the UV levels that the plant is receiving. With this information, LeafLink’s system will be able to use its current conditions as a reference to automatically water the plant based on its needs. 

In this entry of the project, a detailed analysis of LeafLink’s system is provided, including power, compatibility, and timing. Furthermore, all previous diagrams have been expanded on, including a technical schematic of the system, and a detailed software plan that describes the software that will be used for each module.

2. # **Updated Diagrams** {#updated-diagrams}

## **2.1. Updated Body Diagram** {#2.1.-updated-body-diagram}

This section covers all important updates to Leaflink’s body diagram, including each module’s role and purpose within the project. The following subsections group the modules according to their function in the overall system integration. All integrated parts and their relation to the overall system can be visualized in **Figure 2.1** and **Figure 2.2**.

### **2.1.1. Node’s Body Diagram** {#2.1.1.-node’s-body-diagram}

**Control Module:**

* **RP2040 Raspberry Pi Pico:** Manages system control and data processing from sensors.  
* **SPF21371 Water Pump:** Water pump that, depending on the conditions measured by the sensors, will pump small amounts of water to the plant in intervals, according to the plant’s needs.

**Sensor Module:**

* **ST3107X5 Moisture Sensor:** Moisture sensor used to measure the soil’s moisture to determine if the plant requires watering.  
* **GUVA-S12SD UV Sensor:** UV sensor used to measure the environment's UV index to determine how exposed the plant is to sunlight.

**User Interface Module:**

* **ESP8266EX Wi-Fi Module:** Wi-Fi chip that will access the web application hosted by the master to send all relevant data about the plant and its sensors.  
* **Web Based Interface:** Web application that the user can access to see all relevant information regarding their plant and also adjust the plant’s needs.

**Power Supply Module:**

* **B01C4PP8FK Energizer 1.5V Battery (x3):** Battery that will be placed in series (up to 4.5V) to power the system and the water pump.

![][image2]  
**Figure 2.1:** **Block Diagram for Node**

### **2.1.2. Gardener’s Body Diagram** {#2.1.2.-gardener’s-body-diagram}

**Control Module:**

* **ESP32-S3:** Hosts the web based interface for the user.

**User Interface Module:**

* **Web Based Interface:** Web application that the user can access to see all relevant information regarding their plant and also adjust the plant’s needs.

**Power Supply Module:**

* **AC/DC Adapter:** Simple modern charging cable that will constantly power the MCU and its website.

![][image3]  
**Figure 2.2:** **Block Diagram for Gardner**

## **2.2. Updated Schematic Diagram** {#2.2.-updated-schematic-diagram}

## **2.2.1 Updated Node System Schematic Diagram**

The following schematic diagram belongs to the nodes that will be monitoring and controlling the plant watering system. The system is centered around a Raspberry Pi Pico which interfaces with a soil moisture sensor, an ultraviolet sensor, a water sensor, a Wi-Fi module, and a water pump. The Pico is powered by a 4.5V power module.

**![][image4]**

**Figure 3.1:** **Schematic for Node**

**Control Module Schematic Diagram**

The figure below shows our control module. This module consists of a N-MOSFET driving circuit controlled by a Pico I/O pin which will switch the water pump allowing for safe control of the load without drawing unnecessary power from the Pico. When the soil moisture level drops below the specified threshold, the Pico will drive the N-MOSFET gate to a high state which will complete the GND path and activate the pump. The 200 Ohm resistor in this N-MOSFET circuit limits current to protect the Pico I/O, the 10k Ohm resistor pulls the gate to GND to keep the N-MOSFET off, and the Schottky diode works as a flyback diode to protect the system. In this schematic, the N-MOSFET pin 2 is the drain, pin 1is the gate, and pin 3 is the source.  
		![][image5]		  
**Figure 3.2:** **Schematic for Control Module**

**Sensor Module Schematic Diagram**

The following picture shows our sensor module which includes a soil moisture sensor (ST3107X5) and an ultraviolet sensor (GUVA-S12SD) to monitor environmental conditions for the plant. Additionally, a water sensor(DIY-WATER-SENSOR-5) for water tank level monitoring will be used to notify the user if the water tank needs to be refilled. All sensor outputs are read by the Pico’s ADC I/O pins and sensor VDDs will be provided by the Pico’s GPIO, one for each sensor.  
![][image6]  
**Figure 3.3:** **Schematic for Sensor Module**

## 

**Wi-Fi Module Schematic Diagram**

The following figure shows our Wi-Fi module which will consist of an ESP8266 that will provide the Pico with Wi-Fi to allow remote monitoring through a web interface for the user to see. It will be powered using the Pico’s 3.3V pin. The RX and TX ports will be connected to the Pico’s GPIOs. The remaining pins will be left floating.

![][image7]  
**Figure 3.4:** **Schematic for Wifi Module**

**Power Supply Module Schematic Diagram**

The figure below shows our system's power module which consists of three Energizer double A batteries that will supply our Pico with 4.5Vcc. A battery life circuit has been added using resistors and MOSFETs so the user can see the amount of energy the batteries have. One MOSFET is connected from a Pico GPIO to its gate to drive it, meanwhile its drain is connected to a ADC pin that will receive information to interpret the battery level.

![][image8]  
**Figure 3.5:** **Schematic for Power Supply Module**

## **2.2.2 Updated Master System Schematic Diagram** 

For the master mcu we will be using an ESP32-S3-WROOM-1 which will receive the information being interpreted by the Pico and send it to the web hosted user interface. The ESP will be powered via AC/DC wall adapter.

![][image9]  
**Figure 3.6:** **Schematic for Master**

3. # **Bill of Materials** {#bill-of-materials}

The following bill of materials lists all the components required to assemble the LeafLink project. This includes sensors, control electronics, power modules, wiring, and other electronic components like resistors, mosfets, and diodes. Said electronic components could be loaned, therefore offering a slight relief in cost. The estimated total cost of the project is projected to be around $150, split among 4 people it would cost around $37 per person to fund this project.

For access to this bill of material, please click [*here*](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/16_Jxaa2SuneGnDVr3KBJyxtOdiD6hNz_-JqOJPTS5Co/edit?usp=sharing).

![][image10]  
**Figure 3.1:** **Bill of Material for LeafLink**

4. # **Updated Analysis** {#updated-analysis}

## **4.1. Cost Analysis Updated** {#4.1.-cost-analysis-updated}

This segment scrutinizes the economic expenditure within the context of our proof-of-concept architecture. In order to properly assimilate all data, we must first acquire the data for the NRE (non-recurring expenditure) & RP (recurrent pricing). Under the current circumstances in which all NRE are proactively provided to us (thus making *NRE \= $0*), a proper extrapolation from the table (accounting for quantities as well as pricing), gives us: 

NRE \= $0

With a recurring production cost of:

RP \= Costtable \= Totalreal \= $150.83

This means the unit cost (Ucost), assuming volume of 1 unit, will be:

Ucost \= NREV \+ RP  
Ucost \= $01 \+ $150.83  
Ucost \= $150.83

If this product were to be introduced into the market, an investment of $2000 for software licenses like CAD and $10 for testing tools like wires and breadboards could be assumed. This affects the unit cost for the following volumes:

V=10 : Ucost \= $201010 \+ $150.83 \= $351.83  
V=100 : Ucost \= $2010100 \+ $150.83 \= $170.93  
V=100 : Ucost \= $20101000 \+ $150.83 \= $152.84

With this analysis, it can be observed that the more units are produced, the lower the overall cost per unit, reaching the price of the bill of materials.

## 

## **4.2. Power Analysis Updated** {#4.2.-power-analysis-updated}

This section covers all relevant technical details of each component, like their operating voltage, operating current, and overall system power consumption.

1. **ESP32-S3-WROOM-1**  
   * **Voltage Supply:** 3.3V  
   * **Operating Voltage:** 3.0V \~ 3.6V  
   * **Operating Current:** 80mA \~ 100mA, 350mA at peak

2. **Raspberry Pi Pico**  
   * **Voltage Supply:** 3.3V  
   * **Operating Voltage:** 3.3V  
   * **Operating Current:** 20mA \~ 30mA, 300mA at peak

3. **ESP8266 Wi-Fi Module**  
   * **Operating Voltage:** 3.0V \~ 3.6V  
   * **Operating Current:** 70mA \~ 100mA, 250mA at peak

   

4. **UV Sensor**  
   * **Operating Voltage:** 3.3V \~ 5.0V  
   * **Operating Current:** \~0.1mA

5. **Soil Moisture Sensor**  
   * **Operating Voltage:** 3.3V \~ 5.5V  
   * **Operating Current:** 2mA \~ 3mA

6. **Water Sensor**  
   * **Operating Voltage:** 3.3V \~ 5.0V  
   * **Operating Current:** \<20mA

7. **Water Pump**  
   * **Operating Voltage:** 3V \~ 4.5V  
   * **Operating Current:** 150mA \~ 250mA

8. **AA Lithium Battery**  
   * **Nominal Voltage:** 1.5V  
   * **Operating Current:** maximum 2A is enough to handle peak conditions

Every hour, the peripherals attached to the Raspberry Pico will “wake-up” one at a time and run through their respective activity window. These are the peripherals’ active time:

Peripheral: sample\*event time=active time

tUV : 10\*100ms=1s  
tMoisture : 10\*100ms=1s  
tWater : 10\*100ms=1s  
tADC : 5\*100ms=0.5s

Tactive=1s+1s+1s+0.5s=3.5s

The MCU and the Wi-Fi Module remain on during all 3.5s. For each peripheral’s active time, their total current consumption is:

UV Sensor’s Active Window:  
IUV, total=IMCU PICO+IWIFI MODULE+IUV SENSOR  
IUV, total=30mA+100mA+0.1mA  
IUV, total=130.1mA

Moisture Sensor’s Active Window:  
IMoisture, total=IMCU PICO+IWIFI MODULE+IMOISTURE SENSOR  
IMoisture, total=30mA+100mA+3mA  
IMoisture, total=133mA

Water Sensor’s Active Window:  
IWater, total=IMCU PICO+IWIFI MODULE+IWATER SENSOR  
IWater, total=30mA+100mA+20mA  
IWater, total=150mA

ADC Sensor’s Active Window:  
IADC, total=IMCU PICO+IWIFI MODULE  
IADC, total=30mA+100mA  
IADC, total=130mA  
Total current consumed in an hour:

Qtotal=(IUV, total\*tUV)+(IMoisture, total\*tMoisture)+(IWater, total\*tWater)+(IADC, total\*tADC)  
Qtotal=(130.1mA\*1s)+(133mA\*1s)+(150mA\*1s)+(130mA\*0.5s)  
Qtotal=428.1mAs

Iload, avg=QtotalThour in seconds  
Iload, avg=428.1mAs3600s  
Iload, avg=0.119mA

Iin, avg=(Iload, avg)(Vout)(Vin)(ղ)  
Iin, avg=(0.119mA)(3.3V)(4.5V)(0.85)  
Iin, avg=0.103mA

Estimated battery time:

tbat, avg=2900mAh0.103mA  
tbat, avg=28155hr  
tbat, avg= 3.2 years

## **4.3. Timing Analysis Updated** {#4.3.-timing-analysis-updated}

This segment showcases all the considerations for timing-specific functions required for this specific project. Using the Raspberry Pi Pico, we operate under these specific configurations:

Timer Name: RP2040 System Timer  
Resolution: 1 μs  
Width: 64-bit  
Prescaler/Divider: 1 (immutable)  
Comparemax: 4.29x103 s OR (approximately) 71.6 min  
Widthcompare: 32-bit

The compare register will never exceed the timer interrupt by virtue, however the proof is that Widthcompare \= 232 & Widthtotal \= 264 therefore timenecessary, being the time within the compare register, will always be less than timemax, that being the possible amount of counting the RP2040 timer system is capable of. With the preliminary parameters set, the establishment of a dormancy period tier interrupt will determine the length of time by which the MCU stays on a low power modality for. For product purposes, this number would approximate to 1 hour for stretching of battery life while still yielding optimal results for plant subject homeostasis. Ideally, the numerical reasoning of the interrupt is based on the following equation:  
finterrupt \= fclkPrescalerCompare \= 1.00MHz1(3.6GHz) \= 0.28mHz

The other timer interrupt counts the length of time in which the pump stays on based on a predetermined moisture level that shows the “moisture tolerance range” of the particular plant. Separated into 4 types, they’ll determine the maximum/minimum digital number by which the sensor would read in order to decide whether to turn the pump on or off as well as the average time it takes for the soil to be within the median (or middle) of the desired range. For this report, the assumption is that the independent compare value within the equation has 4 default numbers to indicate the amount of time it stays on based on pump parameters of flow rate (120 liters/hr). The options for pump operation based on current level with respect to desired level are 30sec, 1min, 2min, and 5min respectively. In the frequency domain, this would equate to: compare\_values\[4\] \= {30MHz, 60MHz, 120MHz, 300MHz}

The equation once again takes the relational form of:  
finterrupt \= fclkPrescalercompare\_values\[i\]

There’s the added condition that during the watering phase, the waiting timer interrupt is turned off. This is for both synchronicity concerns as well as creating an ease on the load in terms of CPU resource faculties & task management.

Lastly, any Serial Communications considerations are entirely equipped for the UART modality which we’ll be using from the connection from the ESP8266EX Wi-Fi module when connected to the Raspberry Pi Pico. This will communicate with the main communications MCU (ESP32-S3) via a baud rate of 115200 bps.

Those are the restrictions, limitations, and functionality for timing interrupt operations. In terms of demonstrations, we will activate LPM timer for a few seconds to at most a minute in order to showcase its possibility in execution. And instead of 1 sample, we’ll add redundancies for validation purposes, and make it so that each sensor recollects data 10 times in the span of 100ms. This requires each read\_sensors() function to be inside of a basic timer interrupt that simply grabs data 10 times at a frequency of 1Hz/10kHz to create a timer that does an action once every 10ms.

## **4.4. Voltage Compatibility Analysis** {#4.4.-voltage-compatibility-analysis}

This portion verifies that all hardware interfaces within the architecture are operationally compatible without mismatching or incoherent imbalances on a component-by-component basis, as well as the methodologies incorporated to solve any signaling inconsistencies. We’ll divide it into 3 major interfaces: the power rail module, the user interface module, and the control module.

**Power Rail Compatibility**

The charge within the three 1.5V 2900 batteries in series allows it to have a maximum of 4.5V, fueling the Raspberry Pico and the water pump. The water pump remains separate from the Pico in order to avoid any possible noise, as it draws a lot of current when active.

**Control Module Compatibility**

Every peripheral sensor, including the pump, is operating under nominal conditions provided by the MCU’s 3V3 pin.

**User Interface Compatibility**

Every component within this module’s confines: the ESP8266 Wi-Fi module, ESP32-S3-WROOM-1, and the Raspberry Pi Pico all operate under desired voltage of 3.3V. 

5. # **Software Plan** {#software-plan}

## 

## **5.1 The Master** {#5.1-the-master}

The ESP32-S3 operates as the master controller of the system. In contrast to the Raspberry Pi Pico node, which remains in deep sleep and periodically wakes to execute sensing and watering tasks, the ESP32 remains active and is responsible for the system’s communication and user interaction layer. Its software is designed around an event-driven architecture, where incoming client requests, configuration updates, and node notifications are processed as events placed into an internal event queue. This approach allows the ESP32 to handle multiple independent responsibilities in a modular and organized manner.

![][image11]  
**Figure 5.1.1.:** **Flowchart for master**

The master software is divided into four primary modules: the main runtime and initialization module, the web user interface module, the plant configuration event module, and the node receiver event module. Together, these modules allow the ESP32 to establish network connectivity, host the local web application, persist plant configuration data, exchange information with the Pico nodes, and notify the user whenever important changes occur in plant status.

After the web server is initialized, the ESP32 enters its normal operating mode. In this mode, it continuously polls events from the event queue and dispatches them for execution. Each event corresponds to a specific action, such as a web request, a configuration update, or a node status message. This structure makes the master software modular and extensible, since new system features can be added by defining new event types rather than rewriting the entire main loop.

**5.1.1. ESP32-S3 Web User Interface Module**

The web user interface module is implemented as an asynchronous service. Its purpose is to handle client requests coming from the user’s browser and convert those requests into actions that can be processed by the master’s event system. This module is responsible for bridging the external web interface with the internal control logic of the ESP32.

![][image12]  
**Figure 5.1.2.:** **Flowchart for ESP32 UI**

The software begins by waiting for a client request. Once a request arrives, the web interface module matches that request to an intended action. Typical actions may include retrieving the latest plant status, reading current configuration parameters, or submitting a new plant profile. After the request is classified, the system verifies whether it is valid. If the request is not valid, the ESP32 notifies the client accordingly and returns to its waiting state.

If the request is valid, the ESP32 does not directly execute all associated logic inside the HTTP handler. Instead, it adds the request to the event queue so that it can be processed by the event execution system. This is a strong architectural choice because it separates request reception from request processing. The web server remains responsive, while the main event loop handles the actual work in an organized way.

**5.1.2 Plant Configuration Event Module**

The plant configuration event module is responsible for handling changes made by the user to the plant profile or operating parameters, storing the data, and delivering it to the node. The module begins when a configuration event is triggered asynchronously. The first step is to determine whether the plant configuration has actually changed. If no change has occurred, the event completes without further action. If a change is detected, the ESP32 updates its local database with the new configuration values. These values may include the moisture threshold, sunlight threshold, watering duration, wake interval, and other parameters that define the desired behavior of the plant node.

![][image13]  
**Figure 5.1.3.:** **Flowchart for event module**

After updating the database, the ESP32 checks whether the node is connected. This verification has a maximum of five attempts before cancelling its operation. This retry mechanism is important because the node may not always be immediately reachable, especially if it is waking on a timed schedule or reconnecting after a sleep cycle.

If the node is successfully reachable, the ESP32 sends the plant profile configuration to the Pico’s node. This ensures that the next node cycle uses the updated thresholds and watering parameters. If the node is not immediately connected, the retry mechanism continues until the maximum allowed attempts are exhausted. This behavior improves communication reliability without indefinitely blocking the master’s operation.

**5.1.3. Node Receiver Event Module**

The node receiver event module is responsible for processing data and notifications sent from the Pico’s node. Since the node periodically wakes, executes monitoring and control tasks, and then transmits the results back to the master, the ESP32 must be able to receive this information, classify it, and decide how to respond.This module begins asynchronously when the ESP32 receives data from the node. The incoming packet is first parsed so that the master can extract its contents. 

![][image14]  
**Figure 5.1.4.:** **Flowchart for receiver event**

After parsing, the software classifies the notification type. This classification may distinguish between normal system status messages, warning conditions, watering events, or other node-generated results. Once the notification type is classified, the ESP32 determines whether the message is important. If the message does not require immediate user attention, the master stores the node status in the local database and ends the event. This allows the system to maintain a historical or current state record without overwhelming the user with unnecessary alerts.

If the message is important, the ESP32 triggers a user notification through the web UI. This means that warnings such as abnormal sunlight conditions, moisture-related alerts, or significant watering events can be surfaced directly to the user interface. In this way, the ESP32 acts as a filter and presentation layer for the data produced by the node.  
**5.1.4. Event Queue and Event Execution Model**

A central feature of the ESP32 software design is the event queue. Rather than tightly coupling every subsystem together, the master software organizes work into independent events that are queued and later executed by the main runtime loop. This design improves responsiveness and creates a consistent model for handling user requests, configuration updates, and incoming nodes messages.

The event queue is populated by different software sources:

- The web UI module adds events when the user submits valid requests  
- The plant configuration module adds configuration transmission work  
- The node receiver module generates processing events when the Pico sends data  
- The main runtime loop continuously polls and dispatches the next event to be executed.

In this structure, the ESP32 behaves like an event dispatcher. The web server remains focused on communication with the browser, while backend actions are handled independently by the event system. This makes the master more robust and more suitable for concurrent or asynchronous system behavior.

## **5.2. The Nodes** {#5.2.-the-nodes}

The current Irrigation Node software is implemented for the Raspberry Pi Pico using C++ and the Pico SDK. This node is the embedded portion of LeafLink that is physically connected to the plant, so its software is responsible for collecting measurements, interpreting the plant condition, controlling the water pump, monitoring its own supply power, and preparing information that can be shared with the master system. The node is no longer only described as a future set of independent tasks; the repository already contains the task scheduler, sensor drivers, pump controller, Wi-Fi module, flash configuration manager, shared status structure, and the main runtime loop. The design is centered around one `AppContext` object that stores every subsystem used by the node. This context is passed into scheduled tasks, so each task can read sensors, update plant status, request configuration, control the pump, or report errors without relying on a large global control loop. This structure makes the node easier to test because each module has a clear responsibility while still being able to cooperate through the shared runtime context.

```cpp
AppContext context = {
  .moisture  = SoilMoistureSensor(16, 500, 30.0f),
  .uv        = UVSensor(16, 500, 6.0f),
  .water     = WaterLevelSensor(8, 500, 128.0f),
  .temperature = TemperatureSensor(8),
  .pump      = PumpController(),
  .wifi      = WifiModule(uart0),
  .power     = PowerSensor(8, 500, 0.5f, 0.0f, 3.3f),
  .config    = ConfigManager(),
  .adc       = ADCController(adc_enable_channels, 4),
  .scheduler = &scheduler
};
```

A key design objective is still low-power operation, but the actual implementation should be described more precisely as an interrupt-driven wait loop instead of a complete deep-sleep product mode. The Pico configures a repeating timer, enters `__wfi()` so the CPU waits for interrupts, and only drains the scheduler when the timer flag is set. The repository currently uses a 10 second repeating timer for demonstration and integration testing, while the comments show that a 60 second interval was also intended. After all queued tasks are executed, the current `main` schedules the next power-read task and returns to the wait state.

```cpp
add_repeating_timer_ms(-10000, timer_callback, nullptr, &timer);

while (true) {
  __wfi();
  if (!g_timer_fired) {
    continue;
  }
  g_timer_fired = false;
  while (!context.scheduler->empty()) {
    auto task = context.scheduler->pop();
    if (task != nullptr) {
      task(context);
      toggle_led();
    }
  }
  context.scheduler->schedule(Tasks::read_power);
}
```

**5.2.1. Software Architecture Overview**

The node software architecture is organized around a layered design where hardware-specific drivers are separated from higher-level plant behavior. At the lowest level, individual modules know how to read a specific sensor, drive a GPIO, communicate through UART, or access flash memory. Above those modules, the task layer decides the order in which those operations happen during an active cycle. The node software is divided into implemented modules that map directly to the files in the repository. `Main.cpp` owns startup, hardware initialization, the repeating timer, and the sleep/wake execution loop. `TaskScheduler` owns the fixed-size FIFO queue of function tasks. `Tasks.cpp` contains the high-level operations that connect modules together, such as loading configuration, reading sensors, checking plant conditions, running the pump, and notifying status or errors. The sensor modules convert raw ADC readings into useful values for moisture, UV index, water level, internal temperature, and battery power. The communication module uses the ESP8266 through UART AT commands, and the configuration manager persists node parameters in the last flash sector. This division is important because the node must interact with several hardware devices, but the main loop should remain simple enough to maintain and debug.

```cpp
namespace Tasks {
  void load_config_from_flash(AppContext &ctx);
  void request_config_from_master(AppContext &ctx);
  void apply_config_to_sensors(AppContext &ctx);
  void read_power(AppContext &ctx);
  void read_sensors(AppContext &ctx);
  void check_plant_conditions(AppContext &ctx);
  void control_pump(AppContext &ctx);
  void notify_error(AppContext &ctx);
  void notify_status(AppContext &ctx);
}
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Timer interrupt fires] --> B[Main loop leaves WFI]
  B --> C{Scheduler has tasks?}
  C -->|Yes| D[Pop next task]
  D --> E[Execute task with AppContext]
  E --> F[Toggle Pico LED]
  F --> C
  C -->|No| G[Schedule next read_power task]
  G --> H[Return to WFI wait state]
```

**Figure 5.2.1.:** **Updated flowchart for the node main event**

**5.2.2. Main System Initialization**

The main system initialization stage prepares the Pico before any plant logic is executed. This stage is important because every hardware module depends on correct pin setup, ADC setup, calibration values, and scheduler availability before the node can make reliable decisions. The main function begins by enabling standard I/O, waiting briefly for USB serial availability, and constructing the `TaskScheduler`. It then creates the ADC enable-channel table used by the shared analog input system. The current ADC design uses Pico ADC pin 26 as the single analog read pin, while GPIO-controlled enable lines power one sensor path at a time. The configured enable lines are the power sensor, soil moisture sensor, water level sensor, and UV sensor. This design reduces simultaneous sensor current draw and keeps analog reads isolated by making the software explicitly enable only the sensor being sampled. In practice, initialization acts as the boundary between the physical wiring of the node and the scheduled software behavior that runs afterward.

```cpp
const ADCEnableChannel adc_enable_channels[] = {
  PowerSensor::POWER_PIN,
  SoilMoistureSensor::POWER_PIN,
  WaterLevelSensor::POWER_PIN,
  UVSensor::POWER_PIN,
};
```

After the context is built, the software initializes the ADC controller and the active modules. The soil sensor is calibrated with `3000` as dry and `1500` as wet, the water-level sensor is calibrated with `0` as dry and `3500` as wet, and the UV, temperature, and power modules are initialized. The current `Main.cpp` has the pump initialization commented out, and it also has the full startup chain for loading configuration and reading all plant sensors commented out. Therefore, the current running firmware should be understood as a power-monitoring integration build, with the rest of the node behavior implemented in modules and tasks but not all enabled in the main loop.

```cpp
context.adc.init();
context.moisture.calibrate(3000, 1500);
context.moisture.init();
context.uv.init();
context.water.calibrate(0, 3500);
context.water.init();
context.temperature.init();
context.power.init();

context.scheduler->schedule(Tasks::read_power);
```

**5.2.3. Deep Sleep and Periodic Wake-Up Operation**

The periodic wake-up behavior is the software mechanism used to support the low-power goal of the Irrigation Node. Instead of continuously polling sensors in a busy loop, the node waits until a timer interrupt indicates that it is time to perform another monitoring cycle. A major feature of the current node runtime is that active work is grouped into short scheduler bursts. The `timer_callback` does not execute the plant logic directly inside the interrupt. Instead, it only sets a volatile flag, and the main loop performs the actual work after waking. This is safer because sensor reads, UART operations, flash access, and pump control can take time and should not be performed inside the timer interrupt itself. Separating the interrupt from the actual task execution also makes the runtime more predictable, since the interrupt only signals that work is pending while the scheduler remains responsible for executing that work in normal program flow.

```cpp
static volatile bool g_timer_fired = false;

static bool timer_callback(repeating_timer_t *rt) {
  g_timer_fired = true;
  return true;
}
```

The current implementation uses `__wfi()` to pause the CPU until an interrupt occurs. This is different from claiming that the node already performs a complete long-duration dormant mode for 30 minutes or 1 hour. The design still supports extending the timer interval later, but the repository state is a shorter test interval with task execution after the timer event. This makes the current behavior easier to test while preserving the same overall pattern that will be used for longer plant-monitoring intervals.

**5.2.4. Plant Configuration Module**

The plant configuration module exists because different plants may require different moisture thresholds, UV limits, watering durations, sampling counts, and calibration values. Rather than hard-coding all of these values permanently in the node logic, the software groups them into one `SystemConfig` record that can be loaded from flash or received from the master. The plant configuration module is implemented through `SystemConfig`, `ConfigManager`, and the Wi-Fi configuration request task. The configuration is not handled by freezing and unfreezing a task set, as the older design suggested. Instead, a task attempts to load a flash record from the last flash sector using a magic value and checksum. If the record is valid, the configuration is applied to the modules. If no valid record exists, the node initializes the ESP8266, joins the default LeafLink access point, requests configuration from the master, saves the received configuration to flash, and then applies it to the sensors and pump controller. This allows the node to retain its most recent configuration after reset while still supporting updates from the master when needed.

```cpp
void Tasks::load_config_from_flash(AppContext &ctx) {
  if (ctx.config.load()) {
    ctx.scheduler->schedule(Tasks::apply_config_to_sensors);
  } else {
    ctx.scheduler->schedule(Tasks::request_config_from_master);
  }
}
```

The current configuration structure includes moisture calibration, moisture threshold, UV alert threshold, pump run duration, water-level calibration, water-tank capacity, and power-monitoring calibration. Floats are transferred over Wi-Fi as scaled integers to avoid float parsing in the text protocol. The expected format begins with `CFG:` and contains eighteen comma-separated fields, which allows the master to update the node behavior without recompiling the Pico firmware.

```cpp
struct SystemConfig {
  uint16_t moisture_dry_cal = 3000;
  uint16_t moisture_wet_cal = 1500;
  float moisture_threshold_pct = 30.0f;
  float uv_alert_threshold = 6.0f;
  uint32_t pump_run_duration_ms = 5000;
  uint32_t water_tank_oz = 128;
  float power_v_max = 4.2f;
  float power_v_min = 3.0f;
};
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Load config task] --> B{Valid flash record?}
  B -->|Yes| C[Apply config to sensors, pump, and power]
  B -->|No| D[Initialize ESP8266]
  D --> E[Connect to LeafLink_AP]
  E --> F[Send CONFIG_REQUEST to master]
  F --> G{CFG response parsed?}
  G -->|No| F
  G -->|Yes| H[Save config to flash]
  H --> C
```

**Figure 5.2.2.:** **Updated flowchart for plant configuration**

**5.2.5. Scheduler Module**

The scheduler module provides the control structure that keeps the node from becoming one large sequential function. Since the node must perform sensor reads, decision making, pump activation, configuration loading, and notification handling, the scheduler gives the software a simple way to break those responsibilities into smaller operations. The scheduler is the central execution mechanism of the node, but the repository implementation is intentionally small. It is a fixed-size FIFO queue with a maximum of ten pending task function pointers. Tasks are scheduled by appending a function to the queue, and the main loop executes tasks by popping them from the head. The scheduler itself does not automatically check Wi-Fi connectivity, sleep, or retry failed network operations. Those behaviors are handled by the tasks that require them, which keeps the scheduler simple and predictable. This approach avoids the complexity of a full operating system while still giving the node a structured execution model.

```cpp
bool TaskScheduler::schedule(TaskFunc task) {
  if (count >= MAX_TASKS) {
    return false;
  }
  queue[tail] = task;
  tail = (tail + 1) % MAX_TASKS;
  ++count;
  return true;
}
```

This structure means that each module can add follow-up work without blocking the entire application design. For example, `read_sensors` schedules `check_plant_conditions` after it updates the shared plant status, and `check_plant_conditions` can schedule `control_pump` or `notify_status` based on the measured plant state. Error paths are also represented as tasks, since a failed sensor read writes a message into `PlantStatus` and schedules `notify_error`.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Task scheduled] --> B[Queue tail advances]
  B --> C[Timer wakes main loop]
  C --> D[Pop task from queue head]
  D --> E[Task executes with AppContext]
  E --> F{Task schedules follow-up?}
  F -->|Yes| A
  F -->|No| G{Queue empty?}
  G -->|No| D
  G -->|Yes| H[Active cycle complete]
```

**Figure 5.2.3.:** **Updated flowchart for scheduler module**

**5.2.6. Moisture Monitoring Task**

The moisture monitoring task is one of the most important parts of the Irrigation Node because it directly determines whether the plant needs water. The purpose of this task is not only to read an analog value, but also to translate that raw electrical measurement into a plant-level decision that can be understood by the rest of the software. The moisture monitoring task is implemented inside the full `read_sensors` chain. When enabled, the task powers the soil moisture sensor path through the ADC controller, waits for its warmup time, collects multiple samples, averages them, disables the sensor power path, and converts the raw ADC value into a moisture percentage. The implemented calibration assumes the common behavior where dry soil produces a higher ADC value and wet soil produces a lower ADC value. Values at or above the dry calibration become `0%`, values at or below the wet calibration become `100%`, and values between them are linearly mapped. This makes the watering decision depend on a calibrated percentage instead of an arbitrary ADC number.

```cpp
const float numerator = static_cast<float>(m_dryCal - raw);
const float denominator = static_cast<float>(m_dryCal - m_wetCal);
const float percent = (numerator / denominator) * 100.0f;
const bool water = percent < m_threshold_percent;
```

After all plant sensors are read, `check_plant_conditions` uses the `moisture_needs_water` flag stored in `PlantStatus`. If the flag is true, it schedules the pump control task. This is different from the older text that described several high and low moisture branches. The current implemented decision is simpler: low moisture schedules watering, while abnormal UV schedules a status notification.

```cpp
if (status.moisture_needs_water) {
  ctx.scheduler->schedule(Tasks::control_pump);
}
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[read_sensors task] --> B[Enable moisture sensor path]
  B --> C[Collect configured ADC samples]
  C --> D[Average raw ADC value]
  D --> E[Convert raw value to moisture percent]
  E --> F{Moisture below threshold?}
  F -->|Yes| G[Set moisture_needs_water true]
  F -->|No| H[Set moisture_needs_water false]
  G --> I[check_plant_conditions]
  H --> I
  I --> J{Needs water?}
  J -->|Yes| K[Schedule control_pump]
  J -->|No| L[No pump task]
```

**Figure 5.2.4.:** **Updated flowchart for moisture monitoring**

**5.2.7. Sunlight Monitoring Task**

The sunlight monitoring task gives the node information about the light exposure around the plant. Although the UV reading does not directly activate the pump, it is still important because too much sunlight can stress the plant and should be reported to the user. The sunlight monitoring task is implemented through the `UVSensor` module and also runs as part of `read_sensors` when that chain is enabled. The module powers the UV sensor path, collects the configured number of ADC samples, averages the result, and converts the raw value into a UV index. The current conversion uses a 3.3V ADC reference and maps the configured sensor voltage range into a UV index range from `0.0` to `11.0`. If the computed UV index is greater than or equal to the configured alert threshold, the reading is marked as an alert. This separates sunlight evaluation from watering control while still allowing the node to describe the overall plant environment.

```cpp
const float voltage = (static_cast<float>(raw) / ADC_MAX) * ADC_VREF;
return ((voltage - SENSOR_V_MIN) / (SENSOR_V_MAX - SENSOR_V_MIN)) * UV_INDEX_MAX;
```

The implemented sunlight behavior checks for high UV exposure only. It does not currently generate separate low-sunlight and high-sunlight cases. When `uv_alert` is true, `check_plant_conditions` schedules a status notification task. This keeps sunlight monitoring independent from watering, since the UV sensor affects user feedback but does not directly run the pump.

```cpp
if (status.uv_alert) {
  ctx.scheduler->schedule(Tasks::notify_status);
}
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Enable UV sensor path] --> B[Collect ADC samples]
  B --> C[Average raw value]
  C --> D[Convert ADC value to UV index]
  D --> E{UV index >= alert threshold?}
  E -->|Yes| F[Set uv_alert true]
  F --> G[Schedule notify_status]
  E -->|No| H[End with normal status]
```

**Figure 5.2.5.:** **Updated flowchart for sunlight monitoring**

**5.2.8. Pump Control Task**

The pump control task is the action stage of the irrigation logic. After the moisture monitoring task determines that the soil is below the configured threshold, the scheduler can add the pump task so that watering is performed automatically instead of requiring manual user intervention. The pump control task is implemented by `PumpController`. The pump driver uses GPIO 15 as the pump control output. When `run()` is called, the controller sets the pin high, waits for the configured duration using `sleep_ms`, and then sets the pin low. The default duration is 5000 ms, and the value can be changed by applying a received `SystemConfig`. This is a blocking pump operation, which is acceptable for the current simple scheduler because no other task executes while the pump is actively running. The result is a controlled water delivery window that is determined by software configuration rather than by a fixed hardware-only behavior.

```cpp
void PumpController::run() {
  power_on();
  sleep_ms(m_default_duration_ms);
  power_off();
}
```

In the current repository state, the pump task exists but is not active in the running main loop because `read_sensors` is commented out and `context.pump.init()` is also commented out. This means the pump path is implemented for the integrated design, but the current firmware image is focused on timed power monitoring unless those lines are re-enabled. Before pump testing, the initialization line should be enabled so the GPIO is configured before `control_pump` can call `ctx.pump.run()`.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[control_pump task] --> B[Read configured duration]
  B --> C[Set pump GPIO high]
  C --> D[Wait pump_run_duration_ms]
  D --> E[Set pump GPIO low]
  E --> F[Return to scheduler]
```

**Figure 5.2.6.:** **Updated flowchart for pump control**

**5.2.9. Notification and Reporting Behavior**

The notification and reporting behavior is the part of the node software that prepares information for debugging, user feedback, and future communication with the master. This module is necessary because sensor modules should not directly decide how messages are reported; they should only produce readings or errors. Notifications are treated as independent tasks so that sensor evaluation and communication remain decoupled. The implemented `PlantStatus` object stores either a status payload or a message payload. The status payload includes the sample time, pump state, moisture percentage, UV index, water percentage, ounces remaining, internal temperature, battery voltage, and battery percentage. The message payload stores errors such as sensor read failures, pump control failures, Wi-Fi errors, or configuration errors. By collecting this information in one status object, the node can eventually send a consistent packet to the master instead of having each module create its own separate message format.

```cpp
struct StatusData {
  uint32_t sampled_at_ms;
  bool moisture_needs_water;
  float moisture_percent;
  bool uv_alert;
  float uv_index;
  float water_percent;
  float water_ounces_remaining;
  float temperature_celsius;
  float power_voltage;
  float power_percent;
};
```

The current notification tasks print status and error messages over the debug output instead of sending the status payload to the master. The `WifiModule` already includes TCP send support and a configuration-request path using ESP8266 AT commands, but `notify_status` and `notify_error` are presently logging functions. Therefore, the most accurate current design is that network configuration reception is implemented, while network status reporting is prepared conceptually but not yet wired into the notification tasks.

```cpp
void Tasks::notify_status(AppContext &ctx) {
  const PlantStatus::StatusData &status = ctx.plant_status.status();
  printf("[Notification] Status update: Moisture=%.1f%%, UV Index=%.2f, Water Remaining=%.1f oz, Temp=%.2fC\n",
         status.moisture_percent, status.uv_index,
         status.water_ounces_remaining, status.temperature_celsius);
  ctx.plant_status.clear();
}
```

The Wi-Fi module communicates with the ESP8266 using UART0 at 115200 bps. It powers the module through a GPIO, sends AT commands, joins the default `LeafLink_AP` network, opens a TCP connection to `192.168.4.1:8080`, and requests configuration using `CONFIG_REQUEST`. One implementation detail that should be handled during hardware integration is that the current headers assign GPIO 21 to both `WaterLevelSensor::POWER_PIN` and `WifiModule::POWER_PIN`. The software design assumes per-device control lines, so the final pin map should avoid sharing that GPIO between the water-level sensor enable path and the ESP8266 power path.

**5.2.10. Power, Water Level, Temperature, and ADC Behavior**

Power, water level, temperature, and ADC behavior are grouped together because they describe the node's supporting measurements and the shared analog-reading mechanism. These parts do not directly decide when to water the plant, but they are necessary for understanding whether the node has enough power, whether the tank has enough water, and what environmental conditions are present during a reading cycle. The current active task in `Main.cpp` is `read_power`, so power monitoring is the part of the node that is presently scheduled every timer cycle. The power module enables its ADC path, averages the configured number of samples, converts the raw ADC value into an input voltage using the voltage-divider ratio, and maps that voltage into a battery percentage. In the current construction used by `Main.cpp`, the range is temporarily set from `0.0V` to `3.3V`, while the default configuration structure still contains the more battery-oriented `3.0V` to `4.2V` range. This makes the power module useful for both present testing and later battery-oriented operation.

```cpp
const float v_adc = (static_cast<float>(raw) / 4095.0f) * 3.3f;
return v_adc / m_divider_ratio;
```

The water-level and temperature modules are also implemented for the full sensor-read chain. The water-level sensor maps a calibrated dry and wet ADC range into a percentage and then estimates ounces remaining using the configured tank capacity. The temperature module reads the RP2040 internal temperature sensor through ADC channel 4 and converts the measured voltage into degrees Celsius. These values are written into `PlantStatus` when `read_sensors` is enabled, allowing the same status structure to represent the plant condition and the node condition in one record.

```cpp
status.water_percent = water.percent;
status.water_ounces_remaining = water.ounces_remaining;
status.temperature_celsius = temperature.celsius;
status.power_voltage = power.voltage;
status.power_percent = power.percent;
```

The ADC controller is the software layer that makes these measurements safe to run from one ADC input. It initializes all enable GPIOs as outputs, keeps them low by default, turns on only one sensor path before a read, waits for the sensor warmup period, and disables all paths after the sample batch is complete. This design matches the power-saving goal of the node because unused analog sensors are not continuously powered during every active cycle.

**5.2.11. Code Listing and Functional Demonstration**

The code listing and functional demonstration section connects the written design to the actual implementation required by the progress report. Instead of describing the node only at a conceptual level, this section identifies the files that implement each part of the Irrigation Node and explains how the current firmware can be demonstrated in the laboratory. The code implementing the Irrigation Node software is divided across the `src` and `include` folders. `Main.cpp` implements the node startup sequence, timer setup, wait-for-interrupt loop, and task draining behavior. `TaskScheduler.cpp` and `TaskScheduler.hpp` implement the fixed-size FIFO scheduler used by the node. `Tasks.cpp` and `Tasks.hpp` implement the high-level node behaviors, including configuration loading, configuration request, sensor reading, plant-condition checking, pump control, status notification, and error notification. `AppContext.hpp` defines the shared context that connects the scheduler, sensors, pump, Wi-Fi module, flash configuration, ADC controller, and plant status object. This provides a direct trace from the software plan to the files that were implemented in the repository.

The sensor and actuator code is separated by hardware function. `SoilMoistureSensor.cpp`, `UVSensor.cpp`, `WaterLevelSensor.cpp`, `TemperatureSensor.cpp`, and `PowerSensor.cpp` implement the conversion from raw ADC readings into plant and node status values. `ADCController.cpp` manages the shared ADC input and sensor enable pins so that only one analog device is powered and sampled at a time. `PumpController.cpp` implements the GPIO-controlled pump activation sequence. `PlantStatus.cpp` stores either the latest status payload or an error message payload. `ConfigManager.cpp` saves and loads the `SystemConfig` record from flash, while `WifiModule.cpp` controls the ESP8266 through UART AT commands and requests configuration from the master.

For the functional demonstration required by the progress report, the current repository can demonstrate the timed node runtime by flashing the Pico firmware, opening the USB serial output, and observing that the timer wakes the node, drains the scheduler, executes `read_power`, prints the raw ADC value, voltage, and battery percentage, toggles the onboard LED, and returns to `__wfi()`. For a fuller plant-control demonstration, the already implemented but currently disabled lines in `Main.cpp` can be re-enabled so that `load_config_from_flash`, `read_sensors`, `check_plant_conditions`, and `control_pump` run as one scheduled chain. In that demonstration, dry soil causes the moisture module to set `moisture_needs_water`, the scheduler adds the pump task, and the pump controller drives GPIO 15 high for the configured duration before turning it off.

```cpp
// Demonstration chain when full plant monitoring is enabled:
context.scheduler->schedule(Tasks::load_config_from_flash);
context.scheduler->schedule(Tasks::read_sensors);
context.scheduler->schedule(Tasks::read_power);
```

This satisfies the software portion of the report by identifying the updated node software plan, naming the code files that implement the node functionality, explaining the completed module design, and describing a short functional demonstration of the implemented behavior. The demonstration is limited to the Irrigation Node repository state and does not assume that the master web interface sends live status updates, because the current node code only implements network configuration exchange and debug-print notifications.

6. ## **Integration Plan (ps idk if this is 100% necessary but its in the rubric)**

**6.1 Integration order**

Integration will begin with software implementation and verification. Base code will be developed for the scheduler and timers to ensure proper system timing and task execution. Once the core software is functional we can move on to individual module implementation which will be performed in the following order:

### **I. Sensor Module Integration**

The integration process begins with the sensor module, as it provides the fundamental data required for system decision making. First, code will be developed for each sensor, including the soil moisture sensor, ultraviolet (UV) sensor, and water level sensor. Each sensor will be interfaced with the Pico through its ADC channels. The system will convert raw ADC readings into normalized values such as moisture percentage, UV intensity level, and water tank level to interoperable data for the user. 

Once the sensor readings are functional, calibration procedures will be performed to ensure accuracy and reliability. This includes testing sensor outputs under different environmental conditions and adjusting conversion mappings accordingly. After calibration, all sensors will be integrated into a unified sensor task within the scheduler. This task will sequentially read all sensors during the active cycle one at a time and store their values in the shared system context for later decision making.

### **II. Control Module Integration (Pump System)**

After sensor validation, the control module will be integrated. The pump control circuit, driven by an N-MOSFET, diode, and resistor circuit, will be interfaced and connected to a GPIO pin of the Pico and the positive of the pump to the 4.7Vcc. The pump control task will be implemented to activate the pump when the moisture level falls below the configured threshold.

Timing control will be verified using software timers to ensure the pump operates only for the specified duration, this way the plant will receive only the correct amount of water in line with soil need and plant type. Safety validation will include ensuring proper MOSFET switching behavior and confirming that the flyback diode prevents voltage spikes.

![][image21]

### **III. Power and Battery Monitoring Integration**

For the power module, we will be putting together the battery pack first, which is 3x AA batteries in series in a battery holder. The pack will be connected to power the Pico providing a nominal supply of 4.5Vcc. This voltage serves as the main input rail for the system. The Pico internally regulates this input through its onboard voltage regulator to generate a stable 3.3V rail, which is used to power low voltage components such as the sensors. To this, we will be adding the battery monitoring circuit that will be interfaced with an ADC pin using a voltage divider and MOSFET switching mechanism. With this set up, software will be developed to periodically read battery voltage and convert it into a percentage value. This information will be stored in the system context and used to generate low battery notifications. Integration for the power module will be done with voltage 

### **IV. Wi-Fi Module Integration (ESP8266)**

Once local functionality is stable, communication will be introduced.The ESP8266 module will be connected to the Pico via UART communication. Initial testing will focus on establishing reliable serial communication at 115200 baud. The system will then implement data transmission routines to send sensor readings and system status to the master (ESP32). Error handling and reconnection mechanisms will be tested to ensure robustness.

### **V. Master Module Integration (ESP32)**

The ESP32 master module will then be integrated by being connected to a wall plug 5V charger. Then the locally hosted web server will be deployed and tested independently to verify that it can host the user interface and respond to HTTP requests. Afterward, communication between the ESP32 and ESP8266 will be validated by receiving node data.This will ensure the master will process incoming data, store it in its internal database, and update the web interface accordingly.

### **VI. Full System Integration**

Finally, all modules will be combined into a complete system and the full operational cycle will be tested:

1. Node wakes from low-power mode

2. Sensors are well calibrated and collect data

3. Decision logic is being executed

4. Pump activates when needed

5. Data is transmitted to the master

6. User interface updates   
   						  
7. Communication between master and slave is reliable

8. Power consumption behavior

9. Sensor accuracy over time

## **6.2 Verification Plan**

System verification will be conducted at both module and system levels. Each module will first be tested independently for the following:

1. Sensors: accuracy and calibration

2. Pump: activation timing and control

3. Wi-Fi: communication stability

4. Power system: voltage reliability and battery monitoring

After module level validation, integration testing will ensure proper interaction between components. Tests will verify that sensor readings correctly trigger pump actions and that all data is accurately reflected in the web interface. Additional validation will include:

1. Repeated wake/sleep cycles

2. Fault scenarios such as low battery, empty water tank, etc.

3. Communication failure recovery

## **6.3 Member Logistic and Team division**

Each team member will be assigned a primary subsystem to ensure efficient development and integration:

Sensor Module System Designer: 

Power Module System Designer: 

Wi-Fi Module System Designer: 

Control Module System Designer:  Suheiry Rivera Mangome

System Integrator and Design Leader: 

7. ## **Completion Table**

| Module | Summary | Integrated % | Notes |
| ----- | ----- | :---: | :---: |
|  Water Level Sensor  | ADC sensor that measures the level of a liquid | % |  |
|  Moisture Sensor  | Capacitive sensor that measures change in moisture | % |  |
|  UV Sensor  | Sensor that measures UV using photodiodes | % |  |
|  Battery % Sensor  | Voltage divided connected to ADC pin to determine % | % |  |
|  Water Pump  | Water pump that pumps increments of water periodically | 50% | Code and hardware tested. Only missing node integration |
|  Wi-Fi Module  | Module that houses data and UI | % |  |
|  Battery  | 4.5V battery source that powers the Node | % |  |
|  UI  | Displays data for the user, can be modified | % |  |
